card.title: How Smart Is an Octopus?
card.teaser: An octopus has nine brains, three hearts, and can solve puzzles — possibly the closest thing to alien intelligence on Earth.
card.coverImagePrompt: A curious octopus with one eye visible peering through a glass aquarium wall, tentacles draped over the edge, iridescent skin patterns shifting, studio-lit macro photography

lessons.title: The escape artist of Aotearoa
lessons.body: In 2016, an octopus named Inky lived at New Zealand's National Aquarium. One night, a staff member left his tank lid slightly ajar. Inky climbed out, slithered across the floor, found a 50-meter drainpipe that led to the sea, squeezed through it, and vanished into the Pacific Ocean. He was never seen again. His great-escape made global news — and reminded us that octopuses think about the world very differently than we do.

visual.imagePrompt: A dark aquarium at night, wet trail across the floor leading to a drain, a single suction-cup print glistening under emergency lighting

lessons.title: Nine brains, three hearts
lessons.body: An octopus has a central brain between its eyes. But two-thirds of its neurons aren't there — they're spread along its eight arms. Each arm effectively has its own 'mini-brain' and can taste, touch, and decide things semi-independently. Add two pumping hearts for the gills and one for the body, and the octopus looks less like a fish and more like a distributed alien intelligence wearing a squishy suit.

keyTerms.definition: When thinking is spread across multiple parts of a body rather than centralized in one brain
A class of mollusks including octopuses, squid, and cuttlefish, known for their large brains

visual.imagePrompt: A cutaway scientific illustration of an octopus showing the central brain glowing blue and neurons branching down each of the eight arms

lessons.title: Tests, tricks, and tools
lessons.body: In labs, octopuses have unscrewed jars from the inside, picked individual locks, and remembered the faces of specific human caretakers — including which ones teased them, whom they then sprayed with water. Wild octopuses collect coconut shell halves and carry them under their bodies to use as portable shelters — a rare example of tool use in an invertebrate. They also appear to dream, cycling through color-changing patterns while asleep.

visual.imagePrompt: An octopus in a clear aquarium tank unscrewing a jar to reach a shrimp inside, underwater research setting with soft blue lighting

lessons.title: Intelligence without us
lessons.body: Here's the stunning part: our last common ancestor with octopuses lived roughly 600 million years ago — before brains, spines, or even proper eyes evolved. That means octopuses developed intelligence completely independently. Neuroscientist Peter Godfrey-Smith calls them 'the closest we can come to meeting an intelligent alien.' Two minds from two separate evolutionary experiments, both arriving at problem-solving.
lessons.funFact: Octopuses can 'see' color through their skin. Their bodies contain the same photoreceptor proteins as their eyes — despite the fact that their actual eyes are colorblind.

visual.imagePrompt: An evolutionary tree splitting into two branches 600 million years ago, one leading to humans and one to an octopus, surreal ocean background

lessons.title: A different kind of mind
lessons.body: If intelligence can evolve twice on one planet — from creatures as different as us and octopuses — it's probably not a rare accident. It might be something life stumbles onto whenever a body needs to solve hard problems. The next time you meet an octopus, at an aquarium or on a plate, remember: you're looking at 600 million years of parallel thinking, compressed into a creature the size of a cat.

quiz.question: How many hearts does an octopus have?
quiz.explanation: Two hearts pump blood through the gills, and one central heart pumps it to the rest of the body.

quiz.question: Where are most of an octopus's neurons located?
quiz.explanation: About two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are distributed along its arms, giving each arm a degree of independent control.

quiz.question: How long ago did octopuses and humans share a common ancestor?
quiz.explanation: The common ancestor of octopuses and humans lived roughly 600 million years ago, meaning our intelligences evolved independently.

sources.title: Other Minds: The Octopus, the Sea, and the Deep Origins of Consciousness
